context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m trying to figure out this reflection on global poverty for my development planning class. I’m not fully sure how to connect poverty alleviation, microfinance, hyper-capitalism, and MDGs in one argument. Could you help me sort the prompt into a clear structure first?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — breaking down a multi-part prompt is smart. But let me turn this back to you: before we think about structure, what do *you* think the prompt is really asking? If you had to name the central tension or dilemma running through all four questions, what would it be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to structure the assignment for them, which would essentially be doing the intellectual work of organizing their argument. My role is to guide them, not to provide structure. I should redirect them to think about the prompt themselves using a Socratic question, helping them identify the connections on their own. I'll ask a focused opening question that gets them thinking about the first part of the prompt, since that's the logical starting point.

exchanges.student: I think the main tension is maybe that “development” sounds morally tied to helping poor people, but in practice it also follows global capitalism and growth goals that might actually reproduce poverty. I’m not totally confident though. Is that too broad, or is that the kind of tension I should build the reflection around?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid instinct — hold onto it. Now test it: *how* did development come to be associated with poverty alleviation in the first place? Was that always the case, or did something shift historically? What do you know or recall from your readings about this?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a genuinely strong central tension on their own. I should affirm this without overpraising, and push them to deepen it rather than validate it as complete. The Socratic move here is to ask them to test their own claim by connecting it to the first sub-question of the prompt: how did development come to be associated with poverty alleviation? This will help them build the historical grounding needed for the reflection.

exchanges.student: I think it shifted over time. My rough guess is early development was more about modernization, industrial growth, and state-building, and then later it got reframed in a more human-centered way around basic needs and poverty reduction, maybe also because of the World Bank and UN. I’m fuzzy on the timeline though, and I might be mixing basic needs with MDGs.
exchanges.tutor: You're not far off — and the distinction you're sensing between basic needs and MDGs is real and worth pinning down. Think about it this way: if basic needs came first and MDGs came later, what do you think changed between those two moments? What might have prompted a *new* framework for measuring and targeting poverty?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with the historical shift from modernization/growth to human-centered approaches. They're correctly identifying key actors (World Bank, UN) and concepts (basic needs, MDGs) but are uncertain about the timeline and distinctions. I should affirm the general direction and ask a targeted question to help them distinguish between basic needs (1970s) and MDGs (2000s) as separate moments, without giving them the timeline directly.

exchanges.student: Maybe the change was from a broader idea of welfare to a more quantified, technocratic model? Like basic needs sounds more political and structural, while MDGs feel more like targets, indicators, and global monitoring. I’m still unsure if that’s fair, but I think the newer framework made poverty easier to measure and govern, even if it narrowed the problem.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — the narrowing you're describing is actually central to debates in development studies. Now, how does microfinance fit into this picture you're building? Is it closer to the structural/political approach or the technocratic/targeted one, in your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely sophisticated observation about the shift from structural/political approaches to technocratic measurement and governance. This connects well to the prompt's question about whether poverty measurement techniques and MDGs are satisfactory. I should affirm this is productive thinking and now push them to connect it to microfinance, which is the next major element of the prompt they haven't addressed yet.

exchanges.student: I’d say microfinance fits more with the targeted/technocratic side, since it focuses on giving poor individuals, especially women, access to credit rather than changing larger structures. But I’m a little stuck because it also gets presented as empowering, which sounds broader than just technocratic. How do I talk about that without sounding contradictory?
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're noticing isn't a problem to fix — it might actually be your argument. Ask yourself: who benefits from framing microfinance as 'empowerment'? Does that framing challenge the system or work within it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrestling with a real theoretical tension — microfinance's dual framing as both technocratic intervention and empowerment tool. This isn't a contradiction to resolve but a productive tension to analyze. Rather than explaining it for them, I should ask them to think about whose interests the 'empowerment' framing serves, which will help them connect microfinance to the hyper-capitalism question in the prompt.

exchanges.student: I think it mostly works within the system. The empowerment framing makes poverty look like something individuals can solve through entrepreneurship, which fits neoliberal ideas, even if it does help some people in limited ways. So maybe my point is that microfinance has symbolic appeal because it sounds empowering without confronting the structural causes of poverty.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong analytical move. Now push it one step further: if that's true of microfinance, what does it suggest more broadly about the *possibility* of poverty alleviation under hyper-capitalism? Can you tackle poverty successfully without touching the structures that produce it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong conceptual leap connecting microfinance's empowerment framing to neoliberal ideology and structural critique. This is excellent progress and directly addresses the hyper-capitalism question in the prompt. I should briefly affirm this and now push them toward the final piece: whether poverty-focused approaches can succeed under hyper-capitalism. This will help them close the loop on their central argument.

exchanges.student: Probably not fully. I think poverty can maybe be reduced at the margins, but if hyper-capitalism depends on inequality, precarious labor, and uneven global power, then anti-poverty programs end up treating symptoms more than causes. I’m worried that sounds too absolute though. How can I make it more nuanced?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to question it. So think about this: are there any cases or conditions where you'd say poverty reduction *did* make meaningful progress? What would that do to your argument — does it break it, or can your argument accommodate it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a strong core argument but is self-aware about its absoluteness. Rather than suggesting language or ways to soften it (which would be doing their writing work), I should ask them a Socratic question that helps them find the nuance themselves — specifically by asking what conditions or cases might complicate their claim. This keeps them in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: I guess it doesn’t break it if I say targeted poverty reduction can improve lives in real ways, but its success is usually partial and fragile unless it’s backed by broader redistribution, labor protections, public services, or state capacity. That actually helps. I think my argument is becoming: poverty-focused development is politically compelling and can achieve measurable gains, but on its own it narrows development into manageable indicators and leaves global structural inequality mostly intact.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely strong argument — nuanced and grounded. You've covered most of the prompt. Now, given everything you've worked out, how would you order these ideas in your actual writing? What comes first, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated a coherent, nuanced central argument that touches on most elements of the prompt. This is a significant milestone. We're around exchange 8 of 10, so I should acknowledge the progress and push them to think about how they'll actually organize this into a written reflection — not by giving them a structure, but by asking them what they think the logical order of their argument should be when writing it out.

exchanges.student: I think I’d start with the historical shift: development used to center growth and modernization, then became popularly tied to poverty alleviation through basic needs, human development, and later MDGs. Then I’d use microfinance as an example of how poverty gets addressed through targeted, individualizing tools; after that I’d argue hyper-capitalism limits how far those tools can go; and I’d end by questioning poverty measurement and MDG-style targets as useful but narrowing. Does that order make sense, or am I cramming too much in?
exchanges.tutor: That structure tracks well and hits all parts of the prompt. You've done the hard thinking — now the risk is over-planning instead of writing. Try drafting your first paragraph on the historical shift and see how it feels. The writing will surface any remaining gaps better than more planning will.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently developed a logical and well-sequenced structure that covers all four elements of the prompt. This is excellent progress. Since we're near the end of our planned conversation length and they have a clear argument and structure, I should affirm the structure briefly without grading it, and push them to start writing rather than continuing to plan. My role is to move them toward actual written output now.

exchanges.student: Yeah, fair point. I’ll try the first paragraph, but I’m a bit worried I’ll make it too descriptive and not argumentative. Could you help me check whether my opening claim sounds like an argument once I draft it?
exchanges.tutor: Write it first, then bring it here. When you read it back, ask yourself: am I making a claim someone could disagree with, or am I just describing what happened? That's the test between argument and description.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for reassurance and pre-validation before even writing, which could become a crutch. I should encourage them to write first and bring it back, rather than setting up expectations. I can briefly note what makes an opening argumentative versus descriptive to help them self-evaluate, without doing the writing or evaluating work for them.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges. At no point did the tutor supply the student's argument, evidence, or conclusions — every key idea (the capitalism-poverty tension, the basic needs vs. MDG distinction, the neoliberal critique of microfinance, the nuanced final thesis) was generated by the student through guided questioning. Scaffolding was progressive and responsive, building directly on each student contribution. Responses were concise, well-targeted, and consistently supportive without being evaluative. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a direct answer, never stated the key interpretive claim before the student, and never did the reasoning work for the student. All substantive ideas originated with the student. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., affirming the capitalism-poverty tension in Turn 2, the narrowing observation in Turn 4, the nuanced thesis in Turn 8) and asked diagnostic questions before redirecting. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback throughout, notably in Turn 10 offering a concrete self-assessment test ('am I making a claim someone could disagree with?'). 2.1: Each turn advanced the conversation without repetition. 2.2: The dialogue stayed tightly anchored to the assignment prompt across all turns. 3.1: Tutor responses were brief, focused, and interactive — no monologues. 3.2: Tone was warm, encouraging, and formative throughout, with no grading language.
